image_roles.label: Finished dish hero
image_roles.prompt_note: plated finished dish in warm natural light, inspectable texture, no text, no logos

image_roles.label: Serving detail
image_roles.prompt_note: closer serving angle showing texture, garnish, side dish, and sauce

image_roles.label: Ingredient setup
image_roles.prompt_note: key ingredients arranged naturally before cooking, bright kitchen light

image_roles.label: Cooking process
image_roles.prompt_note: mid-cooking pot or pan moment that explains the method

image_roles.label: Table context
image_roles.prompt_note: dish on table with realistic accompaniments and serving utensils

mistakes: Rushing the base before the raw edge has cooked out.
Adding all seasoning early and forgetting to adjust at the end.
Cooking on heat that is too high once the dish should be steaming or simmering.
readiness: The aroma should smell rounded rather than raw or sharp.
Oil, sauce, broth, or steam should look settled and deliberate.
The final texture should match the dish style before you plate it.

regions.name: Northern Nigerian table
regions.description: Hausa and northern dishes built around grains, rice swallows, dried spices, groundnut, baobab leaves, and street foods.

regions.name: South-West and Yoruba table
regions.description: Amala, ewedu, gbegiri, pepper stews, leafy soups, beans, and market foods from the Yoruba cooking orbit.

regions.name: South-East and Igbo table
regions.description: Cassava salads, oha, white soup, bambara nut cakes, palm oil, smoked fish, and cocoyam-thickened soups.

regions.name: South-South and Delta table
regions.description: Riverine soups, palm nut richness, seafood, afang, edikang ikong, and deeply seasoned broths.

regions.name: National party and street table
regions.description: The foods that cross state lines: jollof, puff puff, pepper soup, boli, small chops, and shared celebration plates.

regions.name: Injera and wot table
regions.description: Stews, sauces, and flatbread built for communal injera service.

regions.name: Fasting and vegetable table
regions.description: Vegetable, lentil, and legume dishes that fit Ethiopia's rich fasting-food tradition.

regions.name: Celebration meat table
regions.description: Rich meat dishes where butter, chile, beef, chicken, and spice carry the occasion.

regions.name: Tagines and special mains
regions.description: Slow-cooked, spiced, sweet-savory, and celebratory dishes that anchor the table.

regions.name: Soups, salads, and dips
regions.description: Harira, fava bean soup, cooked salads, and vegetable sides for bread-led meals.

regions.name: Breads and breakfast
regions.description: Semolina breads and griddled breakfast dishes that make Moroccan tea tables feel complete.

regions.name: Rice and one-pot table
regions.description: Rice-centered dishes where the seasoning base, fish, meat, or vegetables cook into the grain.

regions.name: Onion, lemon, and peanut table
regions.description: The sauces and stews that balance sharp onion, citrus, chile, and groundnut richness.

regions.name: Street and sweet table
regions.description: Market snacks, bean sandwiches, grills, and millet desserts.

regions.name: Rice, beans, and street table
regions.description: Waakye, jollof, kelewele, and lunch-counter plates with many sides.

regions.name: Soups, stews, and swallow table
regions.description: Groundnut, greens, fish, palm oil, and fufu-friendly bowls.

regions.name: Coastal and southern table
regions.description: Attieke, grilled fish, alloco, piment, and market plates built around cassava and plantain.

regions.name: Akan and Baoule slow table
regions.description: Sealed-pot chicken and guinea fowl dishes where steam and patience make the sauce.

regions.name: Leafy stew table
regions.description: Ndole, eru, waterleaf, groundnut, smoked fish, and bitter-leaf depth.

regions.name: Celebration and steamed table
regions.description: Koki beans, plantain, chicken, and party cooking that crosses regions.

regions.name: Cape Malay and Durban table
regions.description: Spiced mince, curry, bread bowls, and sweet-savory comfort cooking.

regions.name: Braai sides and sweet table
regions.description: Relishes, custards, puddings, and tea-table desserts.

curated_collections.name: Nigerian Regional Table
curated_collections.description: A north, west, east, south, and party-food route through Nigerian cooking.

curated_collections.name: Northern Nigerian Comforts
curated_collections.description: Tuwo, miyan soups, masa, dan wake, suya, and grain-led dishes from northern Nigeria.

curated_collections.name: Swallow and Soup Table
curated_collections.description: Soups, stews, and sides that make sense with fufu, amala, ugali, sadza, nsima, pap, and other staples.

curated_collections.name: Ethiopian Fasting Table
curated_collections.description: Vegetable, lentil, split-pea, chickpea, and injera-friendly Ethiopian dishes.

curated_collections.name: Moroccan Salads, Soups, and Breads
curated_collections.description: Harira, bessara, msemen, zaalouk, taktouka, and the lighter parts of a Moroccan spread.

curated_collections.name: Senegalese Rice and Yassa Table
curated_collections.description: Ceebu, yassa, mafe, dibi, fataya, and the flavors that make Senegalese meals unmistakable.

curated_collections.name: West African Street Food
curated_collections.description: Plantains, grills, bean fritters, pastries, snacks, and roadside foods from West Africa.

curated_collections.name: One-Pot Family Meals
curated_collections.description: Rice, stews, tagines, and slow pots that can feed a table without making the cook chase five pans.

curated_collections.name: Vegetarian African Classics
curated_collections.description: Plant-forward dishes with legumes, grains, greens, vegetables, breads, and sauces.

curated_collections.name: Breakfast and Tea Table
curated_collections.description: Morning foods, breads, porridge, bean cakes, tea-table sweets, and quick bites.

curated_collections.name: Across Africa Showstoppers
curated_collections.description: Twenty big-table, street-famous, festival, and debate-starting African dishes built for sharing, photographing, and sending someone the recipe link.

menus.name: Nigerian Sunday Table
menus.description: A generous Nigerian table with rice, soup, snack, and something for heat.

menus.name: Ethiopian Fasting Platter
menus.description: Injera with legumes and greens for a colorful meat-free spread.

menus.name: Moroccan Weekend Table
menus.description: A slow main, a soup, and bright cooked salads for bread-led serving.

menus.name: Senegalese Rice Night
menus.description: Rice, onion-lemon sauce, peanut stew, and market snacks.

social_showcase.name: Across Africa Showstoppers
social_showcase.description: A social cooking board for the dishes people argue about, photograph first, serve proudly, and remember after the table is cleared.

jollof-rice-ng.why_it_moves: It is colorful, smoky, easy to serve in a big pan, and naturally starts the friendly country-pride conversation.
jollof-rice-ng.caption: Whose jollof wins this table tonight?
jollof-rice-ng.hosting_move: Serve from one wide tray with grilled chicken, fried plantain, salad, and extra pepper sauce on the side.
jollof-rice-ng.photo_angle: Shoot low across the rice so the red grains, browned protein, and plantain edges fill the frame.

thieboudienne-sn.why_it_moves: Whole fish, tomato rice, and vegetables make a communal platter that looks complete before anyone touches it.
thieboudienne-sn.caption: A Senegalese rice table that deserves the center spot.
thieboudienne-sn.hosting_move: Plate the fish whole over rice and arrange vegetables like a crown instead of scattering them.
thieboudienne-sn.photo_angle: Shoot overhead so the fish, rice, cabbage, carrot, and cassava read as one composed platter.

attieke-poisson-ci.why_it_moves: Fluffy attieke, grilled fish, onions, tomato, and hot piment create a plate with texture, color, and street-food energy.
attieke-poisson-ci.caption: Attieke, fish, piment, done properly.
attieke-poisson-ci.hosting_move: Keep attieke fluffy and warm, then finish with a bright onion-tomato salad and lemon wedges.
attieke-poisson-ci.photo_angle: Frame the fish diagonally with attieke in the foreground and piment visible in a small bowl.

kedjenou-ci.why_it_moves: Kedjenou wins attention because the method is the story: no added water, tight seal, slow shake, deep sauce.
kedjenou-ci.caption: No water, no rush, just the pot doing the work.
kedjenou-ci.hosting_move: Open the pot at the table if you can; the steam and aroma are part of the drama.
kedjenou-ci.photo_angle: Shoot the opened pot with glossy sauce clinging to chicken and vegetables.

kelewele-gh.why_it_moves: Spiced fried plantain is quick, shareable, golden, and perfect for grazing while bigger dishes finish.
kelewele-gh.caption: One bowl of kelewele is never enough.
kelewele-gh.hosting_move: Serve hot in a lined bowl with roasted peanuts or toothpicks so people can keep reaching.
kelewele-gh.photo_angle: Use a close crop that catches caramelized corners and spice flecks.

banku-tilapia-gh.why_it_moves: Charred tilapia, smooth banku, pepper sauce, and hand-eaten serving make it instantly social.
banku-tilapia-gh.caption: Tilapia, banku, shito, and no overthinking.
banku-tilapia-gh.hosting_move: Put shito and fresh pepper sauce in separate small bowls so guests can choose their heat.
banku-tilapia-gh.photo_angle: Shoot the fish whole with banku tucked beside it and sauce catching light.

cachupa-rica-cv.why_it_moves: Corn, beans, sausage, meat, and vegetables give this Cape Verdean classic a generous, cooked-all-day feel.
cachupa-rica-cv.caption: Cachupa rica is the kind of bowl that tells on your patience.
cachupa-rica-cv.hosting_move: Serve family-style, then fry leftovers the next morning if there is anything left.
cachupa-rica-cv.photo_angle: Shoot a deep spoonful lifting corn, beans, and meat from the pot.

koshari-eg.why_it_moves: Rice, pasta, lentils, chickpeas, tomato sauce, vinegar, and fried onions create a high-contrast bowl people want to mix.
koshari-eg.caption: Egypt built the perfect carb stack.
koshari-eg.hosting_move: Keep fried onions separate until serving so the crunch lands fresh.
koshari-eg.photo_angle: Shoot before mixing with onion crunch piled high and sauce streaking down one side.

chicken-tagine-preserved-lemons-ma.why_it_moves: Preserved lemon, olives, herbs, and slow chicken make a fragrant centerpiece that looks elegant without fuss.
chicken-tagine-preserved-lemons-ma.caption: Preserved lemon changes the whole room.
chicken-tagine-preserved-lemons-ma.hosting_move: Bring the tagine or pot to the table closed, then uncover it in front of guests.
chicken-tagine-preserved-lemons-ma.photo_angle: Shoot from above just after uncovering, with lemon peel and olives clearly visible.

couscous-royal-dz.why_it_moves: Steamed couscous, vegetables, meats, and broth create the kind of abundant platter people gather around.
couscous-royal-dz.caption: Couscous royal means the table is not playing small.
couscous-royal-dz.hosting_move: Mound couscous first, then arrange meats and vegetables in visible sections before ladling broth.
couscous-royal-dz.photo_angle: Use a wide overhead shot so the abundance reads immediately.

lablabi-tn.why_it_moves: Bread, chickpeas, cumin broth, harissa, egg, tuna, olive oil, and lemon make a customizable bowl with street-food personality.
lablabi-tn.caption: Lablabi is comfort food with options.
lablabi-tn.hosting_move: Set toppings out separately and let people build heat, richness, and crunch their way.
lablabi-tn.photo_angle: Shoot a half-built bowl with egg, harissa, and torn bread still visible.

doro-wat.why_it_moves: Deep berbere sauce, slow onions, chicken, egg, and injera make it feel ceremonial before the first bite.
doro-wat.caption: Doro wat is not weeknight energy. It is occasion energy.
doro-wat.hosting_move: Serve with enough injera for scooping and place the egg where every plate can see it.
doro-wat.photo_angle: Shoot close so the red sauce gloss, chicken, and egg become the whole story.

injera.why_it_moves: Injera turns a meal into a shared surface, making every stew and vegetable feel connected.
injera.caption: The plate is bread. The bread is the table.
injera.hosting_move: Layer extra rolls beside the platter so guests never hesitate to scoop.
injera.photo_angle: Shoot the spiral texture or a platter where injera peeks under several stews.

kitfo.why_it_moves: Kitfo stands out because it is direct, rich, and confident, with mitmita, niter kibbeh, and soft cheese or greens around it.
kitfo.caption: Kitfo is for people who came ready.
kitfo.hosting_move: Offer the doneness clearly and serve with ayib, gomen, and warm injera for balance.
kitfo.photo_angle: Shoot tight on the seasoned beef with ayib and greens framing the color.

nyama-choma.why_it_moves: Roasted meat, kachumbari, ugali, salt, and hands-on eating make it one of the most social plates in East Africa.
nyama-choma.caption: Nyama choma is not rushed. That is the point.
nyama-choma.hosting_move: Slice the meat just before serving and keep kachumbari sharp enough to cut through the grill.
nyama-choma.photo_angle: Shoot a board of sliced meat with kachumbari tucked close and char visible.

langouste-vanille.why_it_moves: Comorian lobster with vanilla is unexpected, luxurious, and memorable enough to become a conversation starter.
langouste-vanille.caption: Vanilla on lobster, because the islands said so.
langouste-vanille.hosting_move: Keep the sauce glossy and light so vanilla perfumes the seafood instead of burying it.
langouste-vanille.photo_angle: Shoot the lobster shell-on with sauce pooled lightly and herbs for contrast.

bunny-chow-za.why_it_moves: A hollowed loaf filled with curry is portable, messy, nostalgic, and made for hand-held sharing photos.
bunny-chow-za.caption: Durban built the curry bowl you can eat.
bunny-chow-za.hosting_move: Serve with the bread plug on top and sambal beside it so the reveal feels playful.
bunny-chow-za.photo_angle: Shoot the curry spilling slightly over the bread edge.

bobotie-za.why_it_moves: Spiced mince, fruit sweetness, and custard topping make bobotie familiar and surprising at once.
bobotie-za.caption: Sweet, spiced, golden, and very South African.
bobotie-za.hosting_move: Let it rest before slicing so the custard top stays clean and camera-ready.
bobotie-za.photo_angle: Shoot one neat square with yellow rice, chutney, and the golden top visible.

moambe-chicken-cd.why_it_moves: Moambe chicken brings glossy palm-nut sauce, deep color, and a table-friendly richness that makes rice or fufu feel essential.
moambe-chicken-cd.caption: That palm sauce is the whole conversation.
moambe-chicken-cd.hosting_move: Serve sauce generously, then keep extra on the side because people will ask.
moambe-chicken-cd.photo_angle: Shoot chicken half-covered in sauce with a starch catching the edge of the plate.

ndole-cm.why_it_moves: Ndole feels rich, layered, and celebratory, especially when shrimp, fish, meat, and plantain join the plate.
ndole-cm.caption: Cameroon brought greens with authority.
ndole-cm.hosting_move: Balance the bitterleaf and peanut richness with ripe plantain or miondo on the side.
ndole-cm.photo_angle: Shoot close so the greens, peanuts, shrimp, and plantain contrast clearly.
